meta.purpose: Reasoning chains for why a motion decision is right or wrong.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why, tradeoffs, diagnosis, quality review, or strategic choice.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: A motion design system must define token scales before building any primitives.
logic: Without tokens, every primitive decision is arbitrary and unreviewable.
Tokens create a shared language between designers and engineers.
When brand feel changes, only tokens need updating — not every component.
Tokens enable automated lint rules that prevent one-off animation drift.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before writing any animation code in a team context, define: duration-sm, duration-md, duration-lg; ease-enter, ease-exit, ease-attention; and distance-near, distance-mid, distance-far.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: Motion primitives reduce long-term maintenance cost by 10x or more.
logic: Inline animation values in 40+ components create 40+ points of failure and inconsistency.
Changing the brand's motion feel requires touching every component individually.
A well-named primitive lets any team member add animations without deep motion knowledge.
Accessibility fixes (reduced-motion, vestibular safety) apply once in the primitive and propagate everywhere.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Any animation used in 3+ components must be extracted into a shared primitive. Treat inline animation values as technical debt.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: One universal animation duration scale produces uniform, uncommunicative motion. Different interaction categories need different timing scales.
logic: A button hover and a page transition serve completely different cognitive purposes — micro-feedback vs. macro-navigation.
Using the same duration for both erases the information hierarchy that timing is supposed to create.
Category-specific scales let the token system encode intent: 'this is a data animation, it should feel measured and serious' vs. 'this is a UI feedback, it should feel immediate.'
Without category scales, the 'default' becomes the universal choice, and the team loses the expressive range that a motion system should provide.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Define at least four timing categories: Micro (≤150ms, for hover/focus/immediate feedback), Standard (200-400ms, for reveal/entrance), Macro (500-800ms, for page transitions), Data (600-1200ms, for chart draws and counter animations). Each category gets its own token scale. Never use 'Standard' tokens for Data animations — they will feel rushed.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: Motion system documentation that cannot be copied and pasted into a codebase and run immediately is documentation that will not be used.
logic: Motion is inherently temporal — static screenshots of animation are useless.
Engineers adopt what they can copy. If the docs show a code example that doesn't work without additional setup, the engineer will write their own animation instead.
Live previews (Storybook, live docs) let designers verify that the primitive matches their intent before asking engineers to implement it.
Copy-pasteable code removes the 'how do I use this?' barrier — the biggest adoption hurdle for any design system component.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Every motion primitive must have: (1) a live preview showing the animation in isolation, (2) a copy-pasteable code snippet that works when pasted into the codebase, (3) documentation of which token parameters are customizable and which are fixed. The test for documentation quality: can a new team member add a `modal-enter` animation to their component in under 60 seconds using only the docs?

reasoning_chains.conclusion: A motion system without CI enforcement is a suggestion, not a system. CI lint rules are the difference between a documented library and an enforced standard.
logic: Documentation alone cannot prevent drift. Under deadline pressure, even well-intentioned developers write `transition: all 0.3s`.
CI enforcement creates a hard boundary: raw animation values cannot be merged. The only path to shipping is through the primitive library.
This sounds aggressive, but color design tokens already work this way — you can't merge a PR that uses `#ff0000` instead of the design token `color-danger`. Motion tokens deserve the same treatment.
CI rules also protect agents: an AI agent that generates inline animation values will get its PR rejected with a clear message pointing to the correct primitive.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Add lint rules that flag CSS `transition`, `animation`, and `@keyframes` outside of designated primitive files. Add JS lint rules that flag `gsap.to()` and `element.animate()` outside of primitive files (or require a comment linking to the primitive being extended). The error message should be educational, not punitive: point to the correct primitive and its documentation.
